title: Coq au vin
subtitle: Chicken braised in red wine with lardons, mushrooms, and herbs
excerpt: Coq au vin is chicken braised in red wine until the meat falls from the bone and the sauce is rich and deeply savoury. It follows the same method as boeuf bourguignon, with lardons, mushrooms, aromatics, and wine — but takes an hour in the oven rather than three.
sourceNotes:
• Auguste Escoffier, *Le Guide Culinaire* (1903, public domain). Reference for the classical Burgundian braising technique applied to chicken, including the role of the bouquet garni and the browning sequence.
• Julia Child, *Mastering the Art of French Cooking* (1961). Child's version, which most English-speaking cooks encounter first, informs the method here — particularly the separate preparation of mushrooms and the reduction of the braising liquid to a sauce.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes well for up to 2 months. Defrost overnight in the fridge and reheat gently. The sauce may split slightly on reheating — stir over a low heat and it will come back together.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles without adjustment. Use the largest casserole you have and allow extra time for browning the chicken in batches.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the day before, refrigerate, and reheat gently. The flavour deepens overnight.

glossaryTerms.definition: Literally 'rooster in wine'. A French braise of chicken cooked in red wine with lardons, mushrooms, and herbs. Originally made with old birds that needed slow cooking to tenderise; the modern dish uses jointed chicken thighs or a whole cut bird.

To add liquid to a hot pan to lift the fond — the concentrated, browned residue on the base left by meat or vegetables. The fond dissolves into the liquid, adding colour and depth of flavour to the sauce.

content.text: is a braise, and the rules of braising apply: brown everything deeply before the liquid goes in, keep the heat low in the oven, and do not rush the reduction. Chicken thighs are the cut for this — they stay moist and hold up to the hour in the oven that leg quarters need. Breast meat will dry out.

Use the same quality of wine you would drink — the braise concentrates whatever is in the bottle. A Côtes du Rhône, Merlot, or Burgundy all work. The chicken itself is not the difficulty. The work is patience: patient browning, patient braising, patient reduction at the end.
Serve with mashed potato, buttered noodles, or crusty bread. The sauce is the point — make sure there is something on the plate to catch it.
Preheat the oven to 160°C (fan 140°C / gas 3). Set a large casserole over a medium heat. Add {{lardons}} of lardons and cook for 5 minutes until the fat has rendered. Remove with a slotted spoon.

Pat {{chicken-thigh}} of chicken thighs dry and season generously. Brown skin-side down in the lardon fat for 6 to 8 minutes, without moving, until the skin is deep gold and releases from the pan. Turn and brown the other side for 3 minutes. Remove and set aside.

Add {{olive-oil}} of olive oil to the casserole. Cook {{onion}} diced onion over a medium heat for 5 minutes. Add {{garlic}} of garlic and {{tomato-puree}} of tomato purée and cook for 2 minutes. Stir in {{plain-flour}} of flour.
with {{red-wine}} of red wine, scraping up any browned residue from the base of the pan. Add {{stock-chicken}} of chicken stock, {{bay-leaves}} bay leaves, and {{thyme-fresh}} of thyme.
Return the chicken and lardons to the casserole, skin-side up. Bring to a simmer, then cover and transfer to the oven for 45 minutes.

Meanwhile, fry {{mushrooms-chestnut}} of mushrooms in {{unsalted-butter}} of butter over a high heat for 4 minutes until golden. Add to the casserole for the final 15 minutes of braising. Remove the herbs, taste, and adjust the seasoning before serving.

items.symptom: Chicken skin is soft and pale, not golden
items.cause: Pan was not hot enough, or the chicken was moved before it had browned.
items.fix: The skin needs uninterrupted contact with a hot surface to colour. Leave it alone for the full 6 to 8 minutes before turning. If the pan is not hot enough, the fat steams rather than renders.

items.symptom: Sauce is thin at the end of braising
items.cause: The lid was on too tightly or the flour was not stirred in properly.
items.fix: Remove the chicken, discard the herbs, and reduce the sauce on the hob over a medium-high heat for 10 minutes until it coats a spoon. Return the chicken to warm through.

items.cause: Breast meat was used instead of thighs, or the braise ran too hot.
items.fix: Thighs are the correct cut — they have enough fat and collagen to stay moist through the braise. Keep the oven temperature low: the liquid should barely bubble.

items.cause: The wine was not reduced before adding the stock.
items.fix: After deglazing with wine, allow it to bubble for 2 minutes before adding the stock. This cooks off the raw alcohol and begins concentrating the flavour.

content.text: Coq au vin is a French farmhouse dish: originally made with an old rooster, which needed long slow cooking to become edible, braised in whatever wine the region produced. Burgundy gave the name its currency, but versions exist across France using the local wine — Riesling in Alsace, cider in Normandy. The modern dish uses younger birds and thighs rather than old roosters, which means the braising time drops from several hours to one. The technique is otherwise unchanged, and the result is the same: a dish that makes a moderate amount of effort seem like a great deal.
